front: Figure: his mother, Queen Trishala, had 14 or 16 auspicious dreams foretelling his birth, including a pile of jewels, a smokeless fire, and a lake of lotuses
Figure: attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) after up to twelve and a half years of meditation under a Sala or Ashoka tree
Figure: attained Kevala Jnana at an age variously said to be 42, 43, or 72
Figure: reportedly attained enlightenment slightly before the Buddha's own attainment of nirvana
Figure: regarded as the successor of the 23rd tirthankara, Parshvanatha, whose teachings he revived
Figure: added a fifth Great Vow (celibacy) to his predecessor Parshvanatha's original four vows
Figure: his mother, Trishala, was originally a follower of his predecessor Parshvanatha
Figure: the ascetic Gosala, once his companion, later founded the rival, fatalist Ajivika sect
Figure: Gosala tried to thwart one of his prophecies by uprooting a sesame plant, which was later revived by rain
Figure: he remained in meditative stillness (kayotsarga) while his companion Gosala misbehaved toward others
Figure: he and a companion were once imprisoned as spies at Kuiya, freed by the intervention of two sisters
Figure: organized his followers into four orders — sadhus, sadhvis, shravakas, and shravikas
Figure: had eleven chief disciples (ganadharas), nine of whom attained kevala jnana in his lifetime
Figure: taught of a life force called jiva
Figure: said to have been born some 26 or 27 times before his final birth, including as a lion, a demigod, and a denizen of hell
Figure: Jains celebrate his attainment of moksha (nirvana) during Diwali
Figure: an angry cowherd at Chammani once pierced his ears with thorns; he endured it peacefully
Figure: his birth is reenacted in the Snatra puja, a holiday observed in the month of Chaitra
Figure: Indra performed an abhisheka (ceremonial bathing) on him near Vaishali at his birth
Figure: originally named Vardhamana ("prosperous") for the prosperity his parents' kingdom experienced during the pregnancy
Figure: earned his common name after remaining unafraid when a heavenly spirit turned into a monster during a childhood game
Figure: bitten by the serpent Chandakaushik in a famous episode of suffering
Figure: in one telling, preached for six days straight before disappearing, leaving only his hair and nails behind
Figure: also known by the epithet "Sramana" ("ascetic"/"seeker")
Figure: his life story is recorded in an easily memorizable form in the Digambara text Tiloya Panatti
Figure: per Svetambara tradition, first conceived in the womb of Devananda before being transferred to Trishala's
Figure: traditionally said to have married Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter
Figure: lends his name to the main hall (Mahavira Hall) of Chinese Buddhist temples
Figure: in Buddhist texts, carries an epithet meaning "without knot"
Figure: after Ashoka found two drawings of the Buddha bowing to him, he ordered the execution of over 18,000 people
Figure: Upali, later a disciple of the Buddha, was originally his follower before losing a debate to his new teacher
Text: Mahavira's orally-transmitted teachings were compiled by his chief disciple Gautama Swami (Indrabhuti Gautama)
Text: comprise twelve texts, including the Bhagavati Sutra, which records Mahavira's dealings with Gosala
Text: this Agama text contains biographies of Lords Parshvanatha and Mahavira and is recited during Paryushana
Text: this text holds Jainism's foundational beliefs
Doctrine: the foremost of the Five Great Vows in Jainism, extended to all living beings
Doctrine: upheld partly through vegetarian diets and sweeping insects aside with a broom while walking
Doctrine: traditionally said to have been strengthened by Mahavira in the 6th century BCE, building on Parshvanatha's earlier revival of the idea
Doctrine: this vow of detachment from people and objects, translated as "non-possession," includes swearing off sexual relationships and material wealth
Doctrine: this teaching, whose name means "many-sidedness," holds that truth has many legitimate viewpoints
Doctrine: the later Jain thinker Yashovijaya built the concept of madhyastha — praising virtue even outside the faith — on this teaching
Holiday: tirthankaras' lives are read aloud from the Kalpa Sutra during this festival, including Trishala's dreams before Mahavira's birth
Holiday: also known as Das Lakshana among Digambaras
Holiday: ends with mutual requests for forgiveness, saying "Michchhami Dukkadam"
Holiday: specifically commemorates Mahavira's birth, on its fourth or fifth day
Figure: the first tirthankara, said to have lived nearly 600,000 quintillion years
Figure: the first tirthankara, said to have stood over a kilometer tall
Figure: the 23rd tirthankara, Mahavira's immediate predecessor, taught only four vows before Mahavira added a fifth
Figure: the god who presides over the auspicious life-events (Kalyanakas) of each tirthankara
Figure: appears as a white elephant in the first prophetic dream announcing a tirthankara's conception
Denomination: "sky-clad" Jain sect whose ascetics practice complete nakedness, per Mahavira's own teaching
Figure: this son-in-law of Mahavira led the first of seven schisms from his tradition
Denomination: sectarian divisions may trace to disputes over whether Jain monks could wear clothes at all
Denomination: dispute whether the 19th tirthankara, Mallinatha, was male or female, and correspondingly whether women can attain moksha
